points: A tissue is a group of cells similar in structure and function, grouped together for efficient functioning in multicellular organisms showing division of labour. Plant tissues are classified as meristematic (actively dividing cells in growth regions: apical for length, lateral for girth, intercalary for internode elongation) and permanent tissues (derived from meristematic tissue after differentiation and loss of dividing ability).

Plant permanent tissues are simple (similar cell types: parenchyma—living, loosely arranged, stores food; variants chlorenchyma with photosynthesis, aerenchyma with air for flotation; collenchyma—living, flexible with thickened corners for support; sclerenchyma—dead, rigid, lignified walls for strength) and complex (different cell types: xylem—transports water and minerals vertically using tracheids and vessels; phloem—transports food using living sieve tubes with perforated walls).

summary.category: Plant Protective Tissues and Growth Regions
points: Epidermis is the outermost protective layer on young plants (single layer, thin, living cells with waxy cuticle, contains stomata surrounded by guard cells for gas exchange, modified as root hairs for absorption). Cork is the secondary protective layer on mature plants (dead cells with suberin making it waterproof and insulating, replaces epidermis in older stems and roots). Meristematic tissue remains only in specific growth regions—apical meristem at tips ensures growth occurs only at these localized regions, demonstrated by root tip cutting experiments.

Animal tissues are classified as epithelial (tight sheets protecting and lining organs: squamous for thin transport, cuboidal for support, columnar for absorption, ciliated columnar for mucus movement, glandular for secretion), connective (cells loosely spaced in matrix supporting and binding tissues: blood with fluid plasma carrying cells; bone with calcium-phosphorus matrix supporting body; cartilage with protein-sugar matrix flexible at joints; ligaments connecting bones elastically; tendons connecting muscles to bones rigidly; areolar filling spaces and aiding repair; adipose storing fat and insulating), muscular (contractile cells moving body parts: striated voluntary skeletal muscles with multinucleate cylindrical cells; smooth involuntary muscles with spindle-shaped uninucleate cells controlling internal functions; cardiac heart muscles with branched uninucleate cells contracting rhythmically without fatigue), and nervous (specialized neurons with cell body, axon conducting impulses away, dendrites receiving impulses; nerve impulses transmit signals rapidly enabling sensation, processing, and coordinated movement through nerve-muscle functional combination).

Fundamental differences between plant and animal tissues reflect their lifestyles: plants are stationary with dead supportive tissues (sclerenchyma for rigidity) and localized growth regions (meristems); animals are mobile with living flexible tissues and uniform growth. Plant tissues exhibit simple-to-complex organization (from parenchyma storage to xylem-phloem vascular transport) while animal tissues show specialization by function (epithelial for protection, connective for support, muscular for movement, nervous for control). Tissue efficiency depends on cell adaptation: meristematic cells lack vacuoles for rapid division, sclerenchyma cells are dead for rigidity, epithelial cells are tightly packed for protection, smooth muscle is uninucleate for sustained contraction, and neurons are highly elongated for rapid signal transmission across long distances.
